data.title: McDonald's Has A Strategic Business Model And Dividends, But Risks And Overvaluation Exist
data.description:
Whenever I think of a fast-food or quick-service restaurant, McDonald's Corporation (NYSE:MCD) often comes first to my mind. Of course, that is not always the case for everyone else since we have different preferences and tastes. In my defense, I think this is the most popular brand. And maybe it's impossible that you've never heard of it before unless you're living in a very secluded place.

Over the years, McDonald's Corporation has cemented its place in the global restaurant industry. With over 40,000 stores under its name, it maintains a huge domestic and international presence, which makes it a highly competitive player. Its continued expansion remains fruitful amid sustained revenue growth and margin expansion.

However, I have some concerns about its potential overexpansion that may lead to same-store cannibalization. On top of that, external factors are still not favorable at this point. This is why I think MCD has already had a good run, and further stock price increase does not seem justifiable anymore. Technicals are still bullish, but early warnings are emerging.

MCD's Strong Points

An easy answer would be its strong brand recognition. McDonald's Corporation is nearly ubiquitous since it is present on all continents. Considering this, it's not surprising that its revenue remains strong. In Q2 2025, its operating revenue reached $6.84B, up by 5.4% YoY from $6.49B. At first glance, you may think that it was only due to its continued store openings. After all, its total systemwide restaurants increased from 42,406 to 44,113 units as of this writing. But if you look at it carefully, MCD remains in demand and productive. In fact, its average sales in company-owned restaurants reached $1.20M per unit ($4.2M sales/2,054 units) versus $1.15M YoY.

Another thing I like about MCD is its strategic business model due to its high concentration on franchised restaurants. Franchising is not as costly and capital-intensive as company-operated restaurants. They are also less cost-sensitive than adding owned restaurants. So even if inflation remains stubborn, MCD can control cost pressures. As a result, it still enjoys a double-digit operating margin at 47.3% versus 45.0% YoY. Its franchising segment is still well-positioned as MCD also enjoys a higher franchise fee per unit at $100,169 versus $98,918 YoY.

Considering all this, it's not surprising that its operations remain sustainable. As we can see, its operating cash flow remains enough to cover its capex.

Challenges

Despite its strong market positioning, MCD faces several challenges that may hamper its growth.

1. Inflation Remains Stubborn. As a quick-service restaurant, inflation is one of its biggest challenges because QSRs like MCD capitalize on cheaper pricing. As cost pressures increase, it will be hard for prices and sales to offset them. This can squeeze margins if not handled properly. Currently, it's at 2.9%, a notable increase from its stable trend at 2.3%-2.4% before the uptick.

Aside from that, QSRs are unintentionally facing some levels of competition with fast-casual diners because their price differentiation has narrowed due to higher prices on the menu. We know that fast-casual diners capitalize on value propositions like premium ingredients. With that, competition may become more predatory.

2. Unfavorable Consumer Perception May Lower Its Competitive Edge. Despite the popularity and huge global presence, customers do not seem fond of McDonald's. In a recent study, MCD's customer satisfaction index for 2025 is only 70, down from 71 in 2024. It is also at the bottom of burger chains. Its close peers like Burger King of Restaurant Brands International Inc. (QSR) enjoy a relatively high score of 77. Even if we include other chains like coffee and pizza, MCD will still be at the bottom.

If we look at this observation in geographical diversification, Culver's and Burger King lead the group. While MCD capitalizes on brand recognition and expansion, its competitors enjoy high customer satisfaction. This may affect MCD's market positioning and foot traffic in the long run.

3. Overexpanding May Pit Its Stores Against One Another And Affect Sustainability. McDonald's continued store expansion and increased franchising concentration continue to work to its favor. Its revenue and margins were solid last Q2 2025. However, this may not always be the case as inflation still signals economic uncertainty. On top of that, the plan to open hundreds of stores by 2027 may pose a threat to its growth in the long run. While it can increase brand recognition and accessibility to foot traffic, new stores may cannibalize the demand and sales of existing ones. This may hurt not only the company-operated units but also its franchisees. To make it simple, a portion of existing stores' sales will just go to new stores.

The thing is, MCD should ensure the total addressable market. By doing so, it will avoid overexpansion. I'm not saying that it's not doing it since I have no evidence to prove it. But what it's doing right now may be risky. While I understand that it also anticipates economic stability and improved spending in the next few years, MCD may be bleeding out too much cash or borrowings.

If we look at its Balance Sheet, MCD already has over $40B in debt, a substantial increase in the past 11 quarters. The consolation is that 99% of the total amount has a longer maturity. This is supported by its Net Debt/EBITDA of 2.8x, which means that MCD only needs about three years to repay the whole amount at once even if it doesn't have to.

The challenge is that it remains heavily reliant on debt issuance if we look at its cash flow statement. Without this, its FCF cannot cover debt repayments after paying dividends and stock repurchases. Otherwise, it will deplete its cash level.

4. Its Price Cuts Can Broaden Price War And May Squeeze Margins. Another strategy of McDonald's to drive foot traffic is price cuts on the menu. This can help lower the cannibalization risk I explained earlier. It may be confident enough considering its sales growth and margins as it focuses more on franchising than running owned stores. However, inflationary headwinds are still intense. In addition, this may hurt the margins of franchisees. Of course, MCD is not immediately affected since it gets its cut directly from sales. Yet, it must reassess the situation of its domestic franchisees to ensure their capacity to sustain these price discounts.

Valuation

To assess MCD more, I used different valuation methods. The first one is the price ratio using EPS and sales per share. The stock price has been quite reactive with these two financial metrics in some years. For instance, the average stock price increased from $200.83 in 2020 to $234.48 in 2021 and to $253.78 in 2022 when the EPS increased from $2.66 to $2.80 and to $3.13, respectively. The same applies to sales per share. However, the stock price still increased in 2023 and 2024 even when these metrics weakened in those years. Now, it has already reached the $300 mark, which can be justified by the notable increase in the EPS and sales per share.

If we look at its P/E, MCD trades at a very high multiple of 95.58x versus the five-year average of 89.71x. From there, we can say that the market does not seem to care whether MCD trades at a very high valuation or multiple. As long as its financial metrics improve, the price will follow. But again, it's already very expensive. If we use the average value to get its fair value, the target price or TP will be $280.80. The same applies to P/S, which suggests a TP of $270.96. If we add a 5% risk consideration, the TP will decrease.

To have a more forward-looking approach, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. Dividends are another important aspect of MCD. That is why I don't necessarily recommend a sell rating despite its severe overpricing. Its dividends of $7.08 with a payout ratio of just 58.56% show that it earns enough to sustain itself. To do this model, I also included the dividend growth rate of 7.20%. Meanwhile, I estimated its cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. As you can see, I set its risk premium at 7.0%, which is much higher than the typical 5% because of the risks I explained earlier. Lastly, I estimated Beta on MS Excel using the SLOPE function of the daily price change versus the S&P 500. Using all these numbers, the TP will be $284.14. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will be $269.87.

Lastly, I used its Enterprise Value or EV of $268.17B versus revenue estimates for 2025. I expect its YoY rebound in Q1 and Q2 2025 to continue in Q3 and Q4. So, I am setting my revenue growth expectations from 2% to 5%. This will also serve as my reference for my high and low estimates. Using 5%, I derived a revenue of $27.2B. For 2%, the revenue will be $26.4B. My EV/Revenue high and low will be 9.87x and 10.16x. Their midpoint is 10.01x. Meanwhile, the reported EV/Revenue TTM and FWD are 10.29x and 10.03x with a midpoint of 10.16x. If we look at its FWD, MCD is trading quite above the reasonable level. Clearly, it has already priced in my growth expectations. However, it's already a bit too much. If we use the midpoints to derive my TP, MCD should be trading at a maximum price of $295.53. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will be $280.76.

Technicals

MCD's technicals are quite flattish today. The stock price demonstrates rebound attempts at $300 after its recent dip to $293. Now, it is already below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which suggests that the uptrend may not hold any longer. We should note that the most recent high at $317 did not break above the previous high of $320, so momentum may not be strong enough for another rally. The 50-day SMA is flattening again near the 200-day SMA, which means that momentum has neutralized recently.

Its MACD and RSI reflect the price trend. The MACD line is still within negative territory, which means that selling strength still outpaces buyers. But its uptrend after crossing above the signal line and the histogram within positive territory shows the buying splurge in the past four days. This was mainly due to the recent overselling, as shown by RSI. Now, it's at 54, which is already within the normal index range. This can explain the neutral trend of the 50-day SMA. Considering this, there is quite limited room for buying. So, I suggest waiting for another dip or preferably overselling before taking a position. Once the index approaches 70, dips may take place again. With that, investors may have to stay on the sidelines.

Conclusion

McDonald's Corporation stays resilient with its strong brand recognition and strategic business model. However, risks related to intensifying competition, overexpansion, same-store cannibalization tendencies, and stubborn inflation must be considered. Valuation adheres to it as the stock appears overpriced at this point. Technicals are normal, but buying opportunities are limited since the price strength index has bounced back from the recent overselling. I'm recommending a hold rating on the stock for newcomers. For existing investors, they may choose to lighten their holdings to minimize risks and take profit. But selling all may not be a prudent choice due to its stable and sustainable dividends.

data.title: Nestlé: Upside From Improved Costs But Valuation Dictates Hold (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Nestle (OTCPK:NSRGY) reported 9M sales today, and there are a number of items in this earnings report to focus on. The market confirms this, with a significant share price movement today for the Switzerland-native ticker.

The company, like many FMCG businesses, has seen pressure from increased costs coupled with increased inflation and customers having "less money". This has forced many of the companies, not only in Nestle's position but also in grocer positions like Axfood (OTCPK:AXFOY) to be forced to adapt. Overall, I believe the vast majority of grocers on the market in the EU today are overvalued. Many of my positions are divested at this time, with some exceptions for cheap businesses at a high quality, including French Carrefour (OTCPK:CRARY).

If we look at Nestlé's historical valuation evolution, it's very clear that the company's current share price, together with other trends, marks a clear move towards undervaluation in the context of a premium. The question becomes, how much do we discount the company for its current forecasted lack of excessive or impressive EPS growth?

Nestle, which typically grows at least 3-4% per year, has spent the last fiscal dropping 1%. What's more, as of the 9M period, the analyst's current expectations for the company include a close-to-double-digit drop in adjusted EPS towards a 4.34CHF level. This would be lower than earnings have been in almost 5 years. Not until 2026-2027 are earnings expected to improve once again.

Nestle is currently AA-rated, yielding just south of 4%, varying today depending on your entry. My cost basis has me at about flat at today's price, and my YoC is 3.9%.

In this article, I will update my thesis based on the 3Q25/9M results.

Nestlé - Upside remains if you can endure 2025, and wait for upside

The new CEO "came out swinging", I would argue. He's taken an argument of forward transparency. While the nine-month sales growth of 3.3% was good, especially given that it's organic and 3Q results saw the previously negative trends turn positive, there is a lot left to do. In particular, what worries me is the excessive premiumization that Nestlé has enjoyed over time.

It's fairly clear to me that, lacking growth and with inflation and costs going up, Nestlé should probably not be considered at such high premiums any longer.

My last article on Nestle showed me considering "Nestle being on sale". You can find it here, and it's roughly a year ago. However, it hasn't outperformed the SPY, despite a good price and entry for me.

So what has been going on? One of the issues I've had with valuing Nestlé is that it's been very hard to see exactly what the company has planned in terms of savings, targets, and expectations. Things have been opaque - both concrete goals, but above all, how to achieve them.

As of this very morning, we have more clarity.

The company plans to reduce 16,000 people. That's a big chunk, but it does make up less than 20% of the company's total workforce, so that's worth considering. Of this, over 10,000 positions are white-collar employees, meaning reducing SG&A, which I'm sure has to do with digitization as well. This, in turn, is expected to save about 1B CHF in costs, which has the net result of bringing about a higher savings target by as much as 500M CHF. The original plan investors were presented with did not specify the number of headcount reductions. It does now.

The second positive, the company plans to continue its internal growth strategy, which has already improved in terms of momentum and proven to bring about company results.

It's important, in my view, not to overestimate the impact or effect of these quarterly results. This savings target is a good one, but I remind you that this company has been in a consistently negative trend over the medium term for going on 3 years now. Turning around this "tanker in this puddle" will take some time.

The main challenge Nestlé has to overcome is bringing real growth. That's easy when you're a fresh startup, not so much when you're one of the biggest food companies on the planet. Price increases have made the company's products less popular, and the company has reported operational issues, which I have described in previous articles. Furthermore, I will go ahead and firmly argue that the entire food sector is becoming more and more commoditized. In different countries, such as Sweden, private store labels are taking over more and more, and where store labels aren't doing so, smaller brands, which are more local, with less footprint, but far less of a cost base and more agile, are competing with Nestlé.

So, plenty of challenges.

However, Nestlés positives remain a compelling investment argument. The diversity and geographic reach of the products this company offers are second to none. Competition with products and brands like Nespresso is difficult; it has incredible moats. A huge portion, over 55% of Nestlés operating profit, comes from areas where it's evident from sales that the company has not only pricing power, but consumer confidence, and the penetration in this area in terms of private label is close to zilch. No one has come with a "store label espresso pod" - not yet, at the very least. And the company being entrenched in the way that it is, there isn't much that can compete with that.

So, where is Nestlé facing competition exactly - what products can we expect the company to be looking more at?

Pure dairy and VAP-meals (prepared dishes) for one. Volume growth is very clearly not recovering here, but despite this, the company is maintaining its 2025E guidance, which, by the way, I view as being far too optimistic at the time being.

I expect that it will take some time to really bring about better volume growth and reinvigorate the company here. This will be especially hard in underperforming business areas, which Nestlé clearly has (as you can see above). That's why the company is increasing marketing spend to about 9% of sales by the end of this fiscal, in part paid for by the leaving employees. To give you an idea, this was 7.5% of sales in 2024.

Whenever you have a company that generates over $90B in annual sales of products, it takes a lot of money to move that needle - but when a company shifts 150 bps somewhere, that amounts to hundreds and hundreds of millions.

In the end, despite the risks that I view as justified in receiving more investor focus at this time, Nestlé remains the world's largest packaged food company - and this sort of operation neither disappears nor deserves being devalued on the count of some value and pricing pressure. I go by fundamentals. And fundamentals tell us that ROIC has averaged over 16%, more than 100% higher than the estimated sub-7% WACC. The lower returns we're seeing tell us that the premium should drop a bit, but not that this company is worth less than before.

For that reason, I believe that my thesis from 2024 still holds, with some adjustments.

Valuation for Nestlé - Upside is slimmer, but still there if you premiumize the company.

I gave the company a 20x P/E average over time in my last article, which at this time comes to about 100 CHF for 2027E. In this article, I will use the latest volume-pressured results to lower this target by 10%, and down to 90 CHF in taking some headroom for further challenges we're seeing in several markets. This means we're moving away from the 20-year P/E average, and moving to discounting the company's averages, which is the first time I've done this for Nestlé.

On the basis of a 90-95 CHF upside, the company at today's valuation of 82 CHF (at the time of writing this article), no longer has a 15% or above annualized rate of return. However, because I believe the company's fair value is easily, in the long term, 110-115 CHF once they get these things under control, the rating change does not mean that I am selling my stake. It merely means that I am not buying more. I'm allowing for Nestle to "ripen" as an investment at this time, meaning I'm letting the share price appreciate over time until I feel it's time to exit.

So does this mean it's too late to buy the company?

That depends. Are you willing to give the company a premium for its market position and plans for savings and expansion? If so, you may put Nestlé at a higher PT and target than me, and in doing so, justify a higher PT of 100 CHF. Other analysts are doing so, after all. Look at these ranges for PTs from others.

But you will notice that there's not a whole lot of analysts going higher than 100 CHF.

Therefore, I argue that my PT is actually in line with what can be expected here.

I view Nestle, therefore, as a "HOLD", not a "BUY" any longer, and change my rating as of this article.

Risks for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risks for Nestlé remain competition in its market segments where the company does not have a moat, which amounts to 40-45% of its overall income base. In continued and rising competition, the potential for further downside here is not trivial. Many of Nestlés products are further related to either coffee or chocolate, and input cost increases for cacao beans and coffee beans have been high for the past few years. This is likely to weigh on operating margins, as I believe Nestlé is unable to pass on these costs in their entirety onto consumers.

And furthermore, no matter how you "slice" Nestlé, the company is the largest business of its kind in the world. Even if it were to launch a new blockbuster-type 1B CHF product, that would move the sales by less than 2%, and it's hard to come up with blockbuster-type products. What I want to focus on here is that the bigger a company gets, the harder it is for that company to really grow bigger.

Based on this, I change my PT for the company to 90 CHF in terms of PT, and my rating to a "HOLD". This means that for ADR NSRGY, the PT is $114/share.

Thesis

The company is one of the most fundamentally appealing food companies on the face of the earth. The company has one of the most impressive food operations on the planet, and for that reason, I consider it to be a quality business at the right price, though recently, the price for this company has increased a bit too much.

Low debt, a decent dividend, and a decent upside at the right price make this a "BUY" at below 19-20X average P/E.

As of this article, I am lowering my PT to 90 CHF for the native ticker, and also lowering my rating for Nestlé to "HOLD". The company is better waited on here, either to rise or to drop.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (bolded).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough. Based on this, and a price target of 90 CHF/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "HOLD" rating. So, as of October 2025, Nestlé is a "HOLD" here, with a conservative target of around 90 CHF/share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Equity Residential: Buy High Quality At A Discount
data.description:
The market remains near all-time highs as AI-led stocks continue to drive the market. It’s important, however, to keep in mind that the S&P 500 (SPY) is market cap weighted, which means that just a handful of mega-cap stocks are driving market performance. At the same time, I would consider now to be a value investor’s paradise, as many names are trading at attractive valuations with well above average dividend yields.

This brings me to Equity Residential (NYSE:EQR), which I last covered in April, highlighting its strong fundamentals, including a high-income tenant base, low turnover, and strategic positioning in Tier 1 markets. At the current price of $62.09, EQR sits close to its 52-week low with a forward P/FFO of 15.6 and a 4.5% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit EQR including recent business results. I discuss why the recent pullback presents a great opportunity for investors to layer into this quality name for income and potentially strong total returns, so let’s get started!

Why EQR?

Equity Residential is one of the largest multifamily REITs in the U.S., with a focus on high-barrier-to-entry Tier 1 and Coastal markets. It currently owns over 300 communities encompassing over 80K units. Unlike Sunbelt markets, which have seen increased competition in recent years from new supply, EQR’s properties are in supply-constrained regions. This includes population-dense metropolitan areas like Boston and New York City, as shown below, in which there is limited space to build new properties.

EQR also benefits from favorable demographics. This is reflected by the average household income of its tenants at $169,000 annually, which sits well above the $114,500 U.S. average. This means that the average EQR resident's rent as a percentage of income is just 20%. Plus, the gap between the cost of owning a single-family home and that of rental housing currently sits at a historical high. As shown below, the monthly gap of $1,297 sits even higher than where it was in 2006, during the peak of the housing bubble.

EQR demonstrated strong results during Q2 2025, with steady occupancy, strong tenant retention, and effective cost management. Portfolio occupancy was at a healthy 96.6% and blended lease rate growth (on new and renewal leases combined) was 3%. This was driven by 5.2% renewal rent increases with around 60% of residents renewing. Standout performers were NYC and San Francisco, both of which saw strong renewal spreads. This helped to offset weakness in Los Angeles and EQR’s expansion markets in the Sunbelt, which have come under pressure from new supply.

Notably, average household income for new move-ins rose by 8.5% YoY, thereby lowering the risk of bad debt expense. Management noted that just 7.2% of residents who moved out purchased a home, which is among the lowest in recent years. As shown below, portfolio occupancy for the trailing 12 months ending in May sits higher than the prior period.

Looking ahead, EQR is well-positioned to reap benefits as new supply is set to drop nationwide after this year, particularly in key coastal markets. This, combined with the low affordability of single-family housing and cultural shifts favoring renting, should help drive meaningful rent growth beyond this year. New development starts of multifamily units are expected to drop both this year and next. This is after two years of continuous drops to the lowest level in over 12 years, as shown below.

Moreover, EQR is making solid inroads toward cost efficiencies. This is driven by its AI-driven leasing platform, which has halved application completion time. A full deployment is expected by the end of this year. At the same time, a new AI delinquency management tool is being rolled out to enhance payment collection and resident engagement.

Importantly, EQR’s balance sheet is in good shape with limited property developments of around just $200 million. EQR is one of just a handful of REITs with A-/A3 credit ratings from S&P and Moody’s with peer AvalonBay Communities, Inc. (AVB) being the other one. This is supported by a very strong net debt to normalized EBITDAre ratio of 4.45x. This provides safety for the 4.5% dividend yield, which is well-covered by a 69% payout ratio.

Lastly, I see value in EQR at the current price of $62.09 with a forward P/FFO ratio of 15.6, sitting far below its historical P/FFO of 22.6, as shown below.

At the current valuation, it appears the market is overly pessimistic about the impact of new supply and the overall economy. However, new supply trends are demonstrating a decline. Plus, EQR has a strong tenant base and offers a solid value proposition compared to the cost of homeownership. As such, I believe EQR is in a position to offer potential sleep well at night returns in the 10% or higher range. This could be driven by the combination of the dividend yield, FFO/share growth in the mid-single digit range as a base case, and potential for reversion to its mean valuation.

Risks to EQR include potential for economic weakness due to tariffs and inflation, which could pressure rent increases and occupancy at its higher-end apartments. While new supply is trending down, a material drop in interest rates could lead to more new construction. At the same time, cost inflation could pressure margins. Plus, local regulations could delay evictions of non-rent-paying tenants.

Investor Takeaway

Equity Residential presents a strong opportunity for long-term income investors, with it trading near its 52-week low, a historically low P/FFO, and a well-covered 4.5% dividend yield. EQR is strategically positioned in high-barrier coastal markets along with a high-income tenant base and strong occupancy. This provides it with stability amid broader market volatility. With new supply easing and homeownership being less affordable, EQR is poised to benefit from favorable rental dynamics.

Plus, EQR has a conservative balance sheet, operational efficiencies powered by AI, and the potential for multiple expansion. As such, EQR offers attractive risk-adjusted returns for those willing to take advantage of current market pessimism. I rate EQR as a ‘Buy’ for its high-quality revenue stream from a premier portfolio of assets at its current discounted valuation.

data.title: Qualcomm: Competition Limits Its Growth Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) is a designer of semiconductors that supplies big players of the technological industry, concentrating near 60% of its income in mobile cellphones, being one of its star products the Snapdragon. The rest of its incomes comes from the automotive sector, the IoT and the patent license.

Given of the strong competence in its sector, Apple's (AAPL) exit from its customer base and a low estimate of future revenue growth, I consider its making complicated the potential of the shares to recover the ground lost during 2025. By now, I do not see any catalysts for QCOM and I consider that the ideal is to Hold the position.

Strong competition

QCOM hasn't had a good year so far regarding the performance of its shares, with only a 5% increase. This not only represents a lower return than the SPY, but it puts it in the background of the table regarding its main competitors.

The semiconductors industry is playing a key role, in my opinion, in the demand for developing the best possible AI models. In this matter, no doubt Nvidia (NVDA) occupies a prominent place because it controls 94% of the GPUs market, that until now, is the architecture that can most accelerate Generative AI training models (I clarify that QCOM and NVDA do not do the same thing, since they specialize in different areas of semiconductors, but in the general analysis of the sector, I am taking both profiles). Furthermore, the global size of the sector is estimated at $628 billion for 2025, while the CAGR ten year rate is 7.5%, representing $1.2 trillion for 2034.

In that market, although NVDA is a leader in GPUs, it is not the only competitor. QCOM has on par other big companies of semiconductors such as Broadcom (AVGO), Intel (INTC), Micron Technology (MU) or Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), to name just a few. If we take a look at the market share by global income, based on a Gartner analysis, QCOM is in fifth place with a market share of 5% behind SK Hynix, Intel, Samsung and NVDA. If we take a look just at the growth in income of the semiconductors in the USA during 2024, QCOM has finished behind NVDA, AVGO and INTC.

If we focus on the future, the company doesn't show the best perspectives among its competitors. Next, I'll show a chart with a growth rate expected of the main competitors of the semiconductors based on its market capitalization, based on the information given by Seeking Alpha, although I have excluded TSM because it's part of another supply chain (the manufacturing).

QCOM projects a growth rate of 7%, well below its main competitors and only surpassed by INTC.

Why Is Expected Growth Weak?

Given that this is a highly demanded industry, it's worth asking why QCOM has a weak growth rate. Incidentally, this growth rate is not only weak compared to the major players, but also compared to other semiconductor companies that are growing at a large scale, such as First Solar (FSLR), which is projected to grow 23%, or Marvell Technology (MRVL), which is projected to grow almost 20%. QCOM, according to Seeking Alpha's metrics, is ranked 31st in the industry based on FWD revenue growth.

There could be several reasons for the company's lack of strength in showing improved growth prospects, but the main one, in my opinion, is its customers' desire to switch suppliers or create their own chips. This is where Apple's policy of developing its own chips and ceasing to implement QCOM's Snapdragon chipset for its smartphones comes into play, which poses a challenge to its business model. But it's not just Apple; Samsung is also seeking to reduce its dependence on Snapdragon and is trying to increase the performance of its Exynos line. That transition hasn't yet been finalized, and Samsung is expected to continue using QCOM chips for its Galaxy S2 series until at least 2025, but the signal is clear: it would have another impact on the American company.

All of these challenges are impacting the mobile phone segment, where the company reported revenue of $6.8 billion in the last reported quarter, a 7% YoY increase, below the expected level of 10%. However, the company is also showing organic growth separate from its mobile phone business and focused on the automotive sector, IoT, and patent licensing. During Q3 FY25 (June 2025), total revenue was $10.3 billion (+10% YoY), while the largest growth was seen in IoT, generating 24% more than the same period last year: $1.7 billion.

Its automotive segment generated $984 million (+21% YoY) and is the company's other major diversification effort. Part of this effort is evident in the alliance with BMW and the September launch of the Snapdragon Ride Pilot, an automated driver assistance system. To summarize: QCOM aims to be the Android of cars, and these types of partnerships are interesting and will, in my opinion, help revitalize growth prospects.

QCOM CEO Cristiano Renno Amon referred to these developments in the Q3 results presentation, while also:

Our momentum in automotive and IoT is the result of strong execution of our growth and diversification strategy. We remain on track to meet our fiscal '29 target for combined automotive and IoT revenues of $22 billion. We're forecasting fiscal '25 to be the second consecutive year of greater than 15% year-over-year growth in total QCT non- Apple revenues.

He also mentioned some of the new agreements with the Chinese company Xiaomi:

I will now share some key highlights from the business. In handsets, we extended our Xiaomi collaboration with a multiyear agreement. Snapdragon 8 Series platforms will power multiple generations of Xiaomi's flagship devices for China and global markets with volume increasing each year of the agreement. The Snapdragon 8 Elite continues to set the pace of innovation in mobile processors and is leading the transition to AI smartphones with 124 designs shipped or announced to date.

I'll discuss the impact of China on the company's revenue later in the Risks section. But I think it's clear, in my opinion, that the current challenges in a highly competitive sector are dampening revenue expectations and are few catalysts for gaining market share.

Q4FY2025 Projections

Regarding Q4FY25 results, the earnings conference call is scheduled for November 5th, and all indications are that the company will easily beat the market consensus, as has been the case in previous quarters, according to the Zacks forecast, although this does not imply a jump in the stock price because that is not what occurred in the last presentations that beat the consensus.

Revenue estimates are $10.77 billion, which would imply YoY growth of approximately 5%, below the 10% YoY growth of the previous quarter. EPS, meanwhile, is estimated at $2.87, a 6.7% YoY increase, while year-end EPS should be $11.89, which would yield a healthy year-over-year growth of 16%.

However, in the case of annual EPS, Seeking Alpha's estimates in the Earnings section for the coming years are not encouraging, with growth below 2% for fiscal years 2026 and 2027. This is one of the factors that warn of a revenue slowdown in the coming years within the framework of the aforementioned diversification that the company is carrying out.

Regarding the earnings projection by segment, a key element in analyzing the efficiency of the company's current business model and its search for diversification, the QCT Segment (Qualcomm CDMA Technologies), which includes Handsets revenue, will continue to perform well, with estimated revenues between $9 billion and $9.6 billion.

As discussed in the last conference call by Akash Palkhiwala (CFO), and in response to a question from JPMorgan's Samik Chatterjee, the positive forecast is driven by the launches of flagship mobile phone products. The first major impact from the lower market share of Apple phone launches, according to Palkhiwala, would be seen in Q1 FY2026 (December 2025), although this could also be compounded by seasonal factors.

Valuation

To understand the company's value, and given that my thesis is based on future organic growth prospects, I developed two DCF models focusing on EBIT estimates, which in 2024 was $10.2 billion, while in the first 9 months of FY25 (September 2024 - June 2025) it was $9.4 billion. Based on this growth rate, I started the models with an EBIT that I estimate to be close to $12.5 billion as a projection for the end of this fiscal year.

Following the 9.5% EBIT growth estimate listed in Seeking Alpha's Growth section, I applied a 5-year CAGR to that estimated level. In turn, I'm using a perpetual growth rate of 2.5%, in line with the global average. The WACC of 10.2% and the tax rate of 8.1% are derived from Value Investing. CAPEX and D&A are growing at a similar pace as in recent years. Outstanding shares are 1.07 billion, while the base price is $163.

The first model, based on consensus estimates, gives me an upside of just 3.8% with a price target of $167. Under these expectations, Hold is the best option, in my opinion.

Regarding the second model, I looked for ways for the price to reach its all-time highs, which the stock reached in mid-2024, given that some investors could have bought at those levels.

In that case, EBIT would need to double its growth to reach a price of nearly $228, which would imply a 39% upside. But without the fundamentals, even for such growth, I don't think Buy is the best option, even though other multiples suggest otherwise.

Risks

The company faces external risks related to its heavy dependence on the Chinese market, from which 46% of its revenue came in 2024 ($17 billion of the $38 billion in total revenue). This exposes the company to a scenario of geopolitical uncertainty that could lead to restrictions on US exports. A recent example is the antitrust investigation that China's State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR) opened into QCOM for its purchase of the Israeli company Autotalks, which poses new problems precisely in the segment where the company needs to generate solid revenue to offset the weaknesses of Apple's exit or the hypothetical exit of Samsung in the future.

The China case is not the only one the company faces: Arm, the British chip architecture designer, appealed a judge's ruling in a licensing dispute. The last update on this matter dates back to October 1 with this news from Seeking Alpha.

In short, these external risks complicate, in my opinion, the scenario for improving growth prospects. I believe QCOM is a company capable of delivering more due to its robust technological engineering, but at current prices, the valuation is fair. My rating is Hold.

data.title: Bank of New York Mellon: Limited Upside From Here
data.description:
The Bank of New York Mellon (NYSE:BK) has been an excellent performer over the past year with its shares gaining over 40%. The company has been a strong beneficiary of rising market levels, elevated rates, and extreme cost discipline, even as results at its investment management arm remain mixed. The company reported another solid quarter in Q3, though the stock was flattish in response. This may simply reflect how much shares have rallied so far this year with the stock pricing in much of the good news already. Frankly, the stock has consistently exceeded my expectations; I last covered BNY Mellon in June, rating shares a “hold”, but they had added another 20% since then. In hindsight, a buy was merited, and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit BK.

In the company’s third quarter, Bank of New York Mellon earned $1.91, beating consensus by $0.14 as revenue grew 9% to $5.1 billion. EPS was up 25% from last year. The company generated a 36% pre-tax margin, up 300bps from last year as management delivered impressive operating leverage. While revenue was up 7% from last year, noninterest expenses rose by a more modest 4%, given the intense focus on costs.

Balance sheet mix has been favorable

BNY is not a traditional bank that takes retail deposits and makes loans. Instead, it is primarily a custodian. It essentially houses assets and facilitates their movement. Its business is much more operational, as a result, and it is an essential player in the “plumbing” of the financial system. The company ended the quarter with $57.8 trillion in assets under custody and administration, which was up 11% from last year. This growth is largely due to the very strong market appreciation we have seen over the past year. Given its size, I largely expect its assets to track market movements.

Now, when BK custodies assets, it also holds the cash of that client, which is how it has deposits, primarily. BK has $299 billion in deposits, flat sequentially, but up 5% from last year. Importantly, it has $51 billion of noninterest-bearing balances (“NIB”), which are very valuable in this elevated rate environment. These balances are up an even stronger 7% from last year. Increasingly, it appears we have seen these balances bottom out after multiyear pressure. Because of this improved deposit mix, its net interest margin (“NIM”) expanded 4bps sequentially. BK’s NIM should have limited exposure to Fed rate cuts, given a high deposit beta on interest-earning deposits, and depending on quarterly mix, I expect NIM to generally run in the 1.25-1.33% range over the next year.

BK generated $1.24 billion of net interest income (“NII”). This was up 3% from Q2 (primarily due to an extra calendar day) and 18% from last year. While NII accounts for the vast majority of its earnings, BK turned a $616 million pre-tax profit ex-NII. Last year, this was just $477 million. This 29% growth speaks to its operating discipline, and the benefit of higher market levels. Relative to other custody banks like State Street (STT) and Northern Trust (NTRS), I view BK’s ability to generate noninterest profits as peer-leading.

On the other side of its balance sheet, BK’s assets are unlike a traditional bank. While it has $374 billion in interest-earning assets, just $73 billion are in loans. $146 billion is cash, and it has $148 billion of high-quality investment securities. BK runs a liquidity-rich balance sheet, and it takes very little credit risk. It does not have traditional bank branches, and most of its loans are to large corporations. In the event of a credit downturn, I would expect BK’s credit performance to significantly outperform traditional banks. In fact, in Q3, it released $7 million of reserves during the quarter.

Core custody results are strong

Looking to segment results, its securities services fee revenue rose by 11% to $1.45 billion, given increased market levels and higher capital markets activity. After an absolutely massive surge in Q2, foreign exchange trading normalized by 18% to $143 million. Extreme market volatility post-tariffs caused incredible FX volumes, and I am not surprised to see a sharp decline here. Still, FX revenue was actually up 4% from last year. With increased debt issuance as capital markets have unlocked, it is seeing improving issuer services revenue, and I expect continued growth in Q4 and 2026.

Assets under custody/administration in this unit grew by $4.2 trillion to $41.7 trillion. The average fee rate in this business is only about 1.4bps. Custody work is extremely low margin, and it is very much a commoditized service. Ultimately, the operating cost of custodying $1 billion in assets is not that much different than $10 billion, which is why fee rates have become so low. This is also why we are seeing BK deliver meaningful operating margin expansion as market levels and AUM rise, given limited incremental cost. Pre-tax income in this unit grew 26% to $806 million as a result.

Next, Market and Wealth services fees grew 9% to $1.1 billion thanks to strong trading levels, increased treasury client onboard, and elevated collateral management activity. Collateral balances were up about 15%. FX fees here were up 35% from last year, speaking to the fact that while markets have calmed, FX trading is still elevated. While fees were up 9%, noninterest expense was up a more modest 7%, given ongoing efficiency work. As a result, pre-tax income grew by 24% to $875 million.

Investment management results underwhelm

While the custody operation at BK is performing at a high level, its investment division is not. Investment management revenue fell 4% to $546 million while wealth management revenue fell 1% to $278 million. When you consider the fact that market levels have risen considerably over the past year, revenue declines are fairly underwhelming. All else equal, we should be seeing 5-8% revenue growth here. Now on the bright side, BK cut noninterest expense by 5%, preserving operating margins as it shrinks headcount amidst these revenue declines.

The fundamental problem is that while the higher market is lifting AUM, fund flows continue to be very weak. While flows were a positive $1 billion in the quarter, this was due entirely to the growth in short-term strategies, which generally are low-fee. Its long-term active flows were extremely weak, down $25 billion. This has been a consistent trend where we see outflows from its higher fee strategies. As such, AUM is actually down $2 billion from a year ago, and the mix of AUM is also getting worse. Its wealth management unit has posted AUM growth due to markets, but it also is seeing net outflows.

BNY has tried multiple strategies to improve investment management performance, but thus far, not much has worked. It operates multiple brands, rather than one unified brand, and this multi-boutique model does not seem to be working particularly well. We have seen BK sell off brands, like Alcentra, and merge units like Standish, Mellon, and Insight, but results continue to be middling. I continue to question the synergies of pairing an investment management business with the main custody bank. I expect ongoing cost cuts to preserve margins, but I do not see a path to sustained fund inflows, given the persistent weakness we are seeing.

Strong capital enables buybacks

BNY has a strong balance sheet. BK had a tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio of 11.7% in the quarter, up 20bps sequentially. Especially with potential easing of regulatory rules for the leverage ratio, we should see room for BK to accelerate capital returns. It did $849 million of buybacks in the quarter. It has returned 92% of earnings to shareholders this year, and it has reduced its share count by nearly 3% year to date. I see scope for BK to return capital in excess of earnings next year, potentially for a 100-115% payout ratio, depending on the magnitude of regulatory relief. Shares also offer a secure 2% dividend yield.

Valuation is increasingly full

Back in June, I saw BNY earning about $7. With market levels continuing to rise and favorable deposit trends, BK’s earnings power continues to improve, and I now expect closer to $7.25-$7.35 in EPS this year. With ongoing share count reduction and assuming the market stays around current levels, I see BK earning closer to $8 in 2026. That leaves shares 13.5x 2026 earnings. Ultimately, this is a low-growth, highly commoditized business, which makes me feel this is an elevated multiple. Strong cost controls have boosted margins materially, but I expect incremental margin expansion to be more difficult, as most of the excesses have been cut.

Even the potential to reduce capital by ~10% due to eased regulations would provide just $3 of extra value, hardly a game-changer at this point. BK’s business is much less cyclical than a typical bank, given limited loan exposure, so in a period of volatility, investors may value its relative safety. Shares do still offer a 7+% capital return yield, so it is hard to argue shares are particularly expensive.

As such, I am maintaining BK as “hold,” but I see BK moving closer to the “sell” side of the spectrum than the “buy” side as I struggle to see scope for much more multiple expansion here. BK increasingly feels like a “collect the dividend” stock with limited price appreciation. That said, its capital returns can limit downside. I am not opposed to investors beginning to take profits (and would sell if we saw shares get towards $115), but I see no urgency to exit BK.

data.title: SCHD: Your Bet On The Cooling AI Narrative
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

I'm relatively new to Seeking Alpha as an analyst, but I assume that Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD) is as popular here as anywhere else in the online investment community. On Seeking Alpha, however, I see a wider range of different views and outlooks on SCHD and its prospective total return behavior. This ETF is the kind of investment vehicle one can love or hate - the third is not given.

After my own assessment, I think I'm tilting toward the SCHD lovers camp. From what I see, the fund's underlying philosophy, with a strong focus on high-quality dividend-paying stocks with strong fundamentals, perfectly explains the ETF's relative lag to some of its peers in recent years. But this relative lag isn't going to stay forever as SCHD's internal selection criteria has positioned the whole fund quite favorably in the future leaders, the sectors, which will likely stay stronger than the current leaders (mainly large techs) and help value investors protect more of their capital.

I rate SCHD as a "Buy" dividend-focused ETF.

Understanding SCHD's Lagging Performance

It would be unfair to call SCHD a constant under-performer because during the 2012-2022 period, it delivered a massive 316.5% total return, beating its tech-tilted dividend-focused peer, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund (VIG) by an absolute figure of 31.1%:

SCHD vs. VIG (2012-2022 total return performance):

During the COVID crisis, both funds saw a massive capital erosion, losing close to half of all of their gains since 01/01/2012, but then we saw a quick reversal, and SCHD's recovery was actually steeper than that of VIG. One can clearly see that in the above chart.

That steepness to the upside is explained by SCHD's huge allocation to the energy sector, particularly to the high-income generating names within integrated oil and gas producers and midstream companies with a solid track record of payouts. When post-COVID commodity prices started to sharply recover, it fueled SCHD and its overall returns, which led to a subsequent repricing.

When, after the post-COVID economy disruptions and all the monetary stimulus given to the population, the US faced the inflation uptick, and then the Fed started to tighten, we saw a massive hit to the stock market's valuation - particularly so in the technology sector. Both SCHD and VIG fell sharply, but SCHD held up much better. Then the GPT/LLM and AI innovation boom came, and since 01/01/2022, most tech names took off, leading to a massive outperformance of VIG over SCHD and the likes.

SCHD vs. VIG (2022-13.10.2025 total return performance):

Here, we have to take a look at the selection process within SCHD. At its core, SCHD tries to mimic all the changes within the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index, where companies should have a float-adjusted market cap of at least $500 million and a minimum 3-month average daily trading volume of $2 million. Every single selected name should have at least 10 years of consecutive dividend payments. After excluding all REITs, MLPs, preferreds, and convertibles, the index is further left with companies that satisfy four fundamental metrics: Free cash flow to total debt (the lower, the better), ROE, indicated dividend yield, and 5-year dividend growth rate.

What I'm trying to say by calling to the above methodology is that SCHD was actually destined to underperform against names with a heavier tilt to tech. It picks the companies with discounted valuation relative to their 3-year dividend yield. Thus, SCHD effectively ignores the momentum factor, which is very important when one sector within the economy starts to skyrocket. SCHD wasn't introduced before 2011, but I bet it would underperform in a similar manner during the 1995-1999 period.

In addition to that, SCHD limits itself to the side of dividend payment consistency, thus throwing away highly-cyclical names, which tend to revere when the cycle turns up.

Today's Holdings = Future Outperformance

Having built a portfolio of holdings, which were bought at a discount to their 3-year dividend yields, SCHD may be well-positioned during a capital outflow from tech to other sectors, particularly the old-economy sectors like Energy or Industrials. In addition, given that the fund managed to allocate over 16% of its assets to large Health Care stocks like AbbVie Inc. (ABBV), Amgen Inc. (AMGN), Eli Lilly and Company (LLY), and Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK), with each of them having its own prosperity reasons, I believe it gives another tailwind when the asset rotation comes into play.

According to Seeking Alpha, about 54.43% of SCHD's consolidated holdings are allocated to energy, health care, and consumer staples. These three are probably the most beaten-down sectors in relation to the technology sector, based on the YCharts graphs that I've just composed below.

In all three cases, we date back to the early 2000s, when all three sectors started to outperform the technology sector, even though the market still was experiencing the global Internet adoption. Today's environment isn't like the one we saw during the dot-com era, but even if history is not going to repeat itself, the chance of hearing a rhyme seems very high to me.

The areas where SCHD positioned itself today are relatively cheap, and I think it doesn't make sense to download the whole holdings table and go pick-by-pick, writing down each stock's multiples and forward earnings growth. Both of these types of metrics may be misleading, as they depend on what analysts put into their models. The actual growth may differ, and the multiples can rise up strongly even if the underlying businesses don't show anything extraordinary on the operational front.

So, again, the sectors where SCHD is mainly positioned in are historically cheap, in relation to the current leader, the tech sector.

There's a chance that health care, energy, and consumer staples will become value traps, it's hard to deny this type of risk. But we have to keep in mind here that the portfolio composition within SCHD is relatively wide, even across a specific sector. This fact lowers company-specific risks thanks to the addition of the only free lunch on the market - portfolio diversification.

Plus, the majority of SCHD's main industries' relative lags came as a result of multiple contraction. In general, healthcare sector's EPS has begun to recover, and as they keep shifting toward non-acute care delivery and adopt tech and AI-driven solutions to their offerings, this will likely lead to further earnings appreciation in 2026 and beyond. The energy sector's growth remains below sector average, based on CSI markets data, but it's set to improve next year, according to FactSet's analysis:

I think the odds of energy sector's valuation repricing to the upside ahead of 2026 are quite high because the market is usually looking beyond TTM figures.

Also, the current positioning in tech raise concerns, and when more investors start to reposition into sectors with high odds of recovery potential in 2026, I think energy and health care will become the primary beneficiaries.

I'm not expecting the current AI boom to fade tomorrow, but for a wiser portfolio management approach, I'd recommend buying some SCHD as it may finally start to rebound even before that potential fade comes.

Here, it's also important to talk about the main feature of the fund - its dividend yield.

Seeking Alpha's dividend scorecard rates SCHD as an "A+" stock because it's ranking higher than a median ETF on all key dividend-related metrics:

As of 10/15/2025, SCHD has a dividend yield of 3.85%, with an average DY of 3.78% for this year, and the average 2015-2024 DY of ~3.08%:

I think that given the fund's long-term dividend growth of 9-12% for a very long time, I think SCHD is underpriced for what it can offer to its investors.

We can derive SCHD's fair value leaning on the inverse relationship between the ETF's price and the dividend yield (the higher the ETF's price, the lower the yield). If the Fed keeps lowering the rates, it's likely to push a lot of income-seeking investors out of treasuries, and lead them to other options. SCHD's dividend of 3.85% will look very tempting, I don't doubt it.

Assuming that SCHD's dividend will rise by 10% next year, and the yield itself corrects to its historical 3.09% figure, all else being equal, SCHD's price should adjust higher next year. With these inputs, I get a target price of ~$36.82, and it's 37% higher than the current price of SCHD.

So, while SCHD buyers wait for the nominal price rebound driven by the assets rotation, they'll be getting nice dividends.

Risks To My Views

There's some element of timing the market in my today's article on SCHD because I try to predict the fund's future performance. I know that forecasting is a core of any investment research article, but when it comes to ETF analysis, it's about funds' allocation strategies, probably past years' momentum, the market's environment, and thing like these. SCHD isn't the ETF that can brag about its top-notch performance relative to some other dividend-focused peers with a heavier weight allocated to the tech sector right now.

The lag I'm expecting to fade at some point may stay with SCHD for way longer than by bullish case has described. If so, value-seeking ETF investors, who decide to buy the slight YTD dip in SHCD may face further underperformance and losses on an absolute basis.

Also, there may be a better time to buy SCHD when it starts to trade an discount to its NAV again (now SCHD is priced with a slight premium, so buyers effectively overpay for it).

Conclusion

Based on what I stated as my main argumentation, I think it's possible to conclude that SCHD may actually become one's solid bet on a possible cooling of today's AI bullish narrative. I mean, without belittling the current AI innovation cycle, which can be more than Internet in its positive effect on the economy, I think we're getting close to an institutional capital rotation from tech to cheaper sectors and niches within those sectors, which AI hasn't influenced yet much, but where it can trace somehow.

I rate SCHD as a "Buy".

data.title: COPX: Copper Could Become AI Gold
data.description:
Thesis

One of my better picks in recent days has been Fluor Corporation, an engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) company that specializes in advanced projects, like setting up pharmaceutical manufacturing. Fluor also constructs data centers, although it doesn’t rank among the biggest players in that field yet. As far as I have found so far, most of the leaders in data center construction are privately held. Still, amid the AI boom, we can expect data center construction to expand and enjoy tailwinds. This, in turn, should provide tailwinds for crucial data center materials, including copper.

Data centers alone will provide considerable tailwinds. That said, we must be careful not to overestimate the impact. Currently, data centers account for only about 2% of copper consumption, but analysts predict that by 2050 they may consume as much as 9%. By itself, 9% may not move the needle massively. However, electric vehicles consume 3X as much copper as internal combustion engine vehicles and EV sales are rising globally.

I also don’t think we can rule out data centers gobbling up even more copper than expected. China is still playing catch-up in the AI race, but I doubt they will concede. Indeed, I believe there’s a good chance that China will adopt an even more proactive approach in building AI centers, which could drive demand for copper.

In light of all of the above, I rate the Global X Copper Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:COPX) a buy but suggest keeping investments moderate.

Data Centers Consume Vast Quantities Of Copper

Copper is one of the best conductors of electricity. In power lines and large-scale energy grids, aluminum is often favored because it is substantially lighter, among other things. Yet when it comes to smaller facilities, copper is often king. With data centers being built out the world over, vast amounts of copper will be needed. One study found that every megawatt of applied power at a data center will require roughly 27 tonnes of copper. Currently, AI data centers equal to or greater than 200MW are becoming common, which should consume more than 5,000 tonnes of copper.

Further, data centers can’t simply be thrown out into a field and must obviously be connected to some sort of power source. When local grid networks need to be expanded, demand for copper should increase. While aluminum is favored in many grid applications, copper demand should still receive a boost. Indeed, in 2025, about 12.52 million tons of copper were used in grid infrastructure, and consumption is projected to hit 14.87 million metric tons by 2030. Many companies building data centers are also looking at separate, off-grid power solutions, including nuclear power, solar panels, and wind turbines. If power sources are placed near data centers, copper may be favored owing to its better conductivity.

Whatever direction is taken, expanding the energy grid is looking crucial, with industry experts warning of bottlenecks. I expect these constraints to result in substantial public and private investments. Renewables may enjoy a boost as well, and wind turbines, solar panels, and other technologies often use high levels of copper.

Watch For China’s AI Investments To Ramp Up

Then there’s China. American curbs on chip exports, including exports abroad that use American technologies and IP, have slowed China’s efforts in the AI game. However, China is getting increasingly aggressive in leveraging rare earths and other forms of leverage to pressure the United States. Given China’s chokehold over rare earths, which are crucial for defense and technology supply chains and production, I believe the United States will be forced to offer serious concessions, and chips will likely be a major factor. This could produce a flood of semiconductor orders and data center build-outs.

Interestingly enough, however, efforts to shift away from American chips are gaining steam. The Chinese Communist Party (CCP) has banned major tech companies from using Nvidia chips and instead is pushing for the development of homegrown solutions. China isn’t going to catch up overnight, but I expect it to focus on quantity over quality for right now. China’s processors may not be able to go toe to toe any time soon with the best American chips, but they may be able to close the gap by building bigger data centers.

Crucially, China’s energy grid is believed to have substantially more capacity to support AI and data centers. This may reduce the need to prioritize energy efficiency and may allow China to simply outbuild the United States.

EVs Need Copper Too

As already mentioned, EVs use 3X more copper than an ICE vehicle. Electric vehicles now account for the majority of new car sales in China. In Europe, EV auto sales are up ~25% YOY (first half). On a global scale, EV sales are up nearly 30%. With Chinese EV companies like BYD rapidly expanding globally, I suspect that electric vehicles will gain ground in many other countries. Budget-friendly Chinese autos could make car ownership more attainable in emerging markets, thus fueling building growth.

Global X Copper Miners ETF Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

First, I believe that geographical diversification is prudent. Over 90% of COPX’s equity holdings are non-American assets, according to Morningstar. Many companies also maintain global footprints outside of where they are headquartered. So while COPX may not intentionally position itself as a foreign ETF, it does offer a lot of overseas exposure. I find this attractive, especially amid global trade tensions. I believe a well-diversified portfolio will offer (relatively) lower risks and higher upside should the world start to decouple.

With ETFs, I often look at individual valuation and fundamental considerations of major holdings. However, none of the 40 equity holdings top 6% of weight (the highest is 5.5%). With that in mind, I believe it’s crucial to step back to look at the portfolio-wide numbers. COPX’s PE ratio is a rather enticing 16.88, a bit below the category median of 17.64 (Morningstar). This ratio strikes me as rather favorable, given that copper could enjoy solid demand growth in the years ahead. The Price/Sales ratio is also quite attractive at 0.97 (ibid.). Commodities often do carry lower PS ratios but this is still well below the category average of 1.39 (ibid.). Cash flow growth is moderate at 1.08%, but that’s far better than the -6.55 category average (ibid.).

COPX has a pretty high adjusted expense ratio at .65% (Morningstar) but this in line with the .65% expenses for the Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL, Seeking Alpha). The Sprott Copper Miners ETF (COPP) also has a .65% expense ratio (Seeking Alpha). Risks for these ETFs are currently graded as a D- by Seeking Alpha. With COPX there is significant short-selling activity at 8.40%, compared to 4.69% short-selling for SIL (Seeking Alpha). This gap doesn't worry me as silver has been a hot commodity to hedge against inflation, similar to gold.

Still, COPX is a relatively risky investment. Tracking errors are especially a sore spot, with the 3-year at 26.23%, which is actually much better than SIL's 34.98%. COPP is a bit better on this front at 23.95%, but this ETF has only been on the market for about a year, so I'll go with the longer-established ETF. COPP is also more highly concentrated with the top 10 holdings accounting for more than 70% of the portfolio. SIL is even more heavily concentrated with the top 10 holdings accounting for more than 75%. With this type of play based on broad tailwinds, I prefer high diversification. Ultimately, I believe upside justifies the risk for COPX, but would keep investments moderate.

The dividend yield (TTM) is only 1.29% and growth rates are all over the place. The TTM growth rate surged to nearly 42%, but the 3-year CAGR is nearly 11% in the red. Still, the fund has paid dividends for 14 straight years, well above the category average of 4 years. Unfortunately, however, this is a rather expensive ETF in terms of fees, with the adjusted expense ratio a rather hefty 0.650%. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools ding the expenses with rather mediocre marks, but overall, like me, the tools give a buy rating. SIL, for comparison has a dividend yield of just 1.12% (Seeking Alpha).

Why I Could Be Wrong

If the economy goes into recession, construction projects will likely slow at least temporarily, which could deflate COPX’s price. Construction is copper-intensive, and construction tends to be rather cyclical. Meanwhile, should the AI bubble pop, the data center thesis will probably go out the window (at least for a time). I do think AI is getting bubbly, but I don’t think it’ll pop any time soon. While electricity is a rather mature technology, I don’t think we can fully write off technological breakthroughs either. Copper was once pretty popular for transferring data, for example, but is being phased out in favor of fiber optics in key areas.

Key Takeaway: COPX Looks Like A Buy

All-in-all, I believe COPX will enjoy AI-related tailwinds. Add in energy grid upgrades and electric vehicles, and there are enough tailwinds here for me to give COPX a buy rating. That said, the tracking errors are a sore spot, even if they are somewhat common with commodity ETFs. I would keep investments moderate. I view this as a moderate risk investment with good upside.

data.title: Check Point Software: Growth Awaits Management's Drive To Push Its Go-To-Market Strategy
data.description:
Check Point Software Technologies (NASDAQ:CHKP) will be reporting q3’25 earnings on October 28, 2025 before market open. Analysts are relatively mixed on expectations, showing 18x EPS revisions up and 14x down in the last 90 days. There are a number of factors that I believe will determine investors’ reactions following the earnings release, including updates on Check Point’s AI strategy, its go-to-market strategy, and further discounting of its platform services. Given the strong opportunity for AI security and Check Point’s engagement in building its arsenal of AI security applications, I am recommending CHKP shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $198/share at 17.10x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Check Point Software Technologies Operations

Check Point operates under 4 core principles:

  1. Securing the connectivity fabric
  2. Prevention-first ethos
  3. Open platform philosophy
  4. AI-first security

Looking at eq3’25, investors will be looking for growth in billings as Check Point aims to accelerate growth. Management has indicated a focus in advancing Check Point’s go-to-market strategy, which may not be apparent in the historical financials given the flat sequential sales & marketing operating expense in q2’25.

The other driver for growing billings will be new product offerings across email, ERM, and SASE, as well as the unified platform. With the notion of growing the top line by reinvesting in the organization, Check Point may experience modest margin compression in the near term before realizing stronger operating leverage.

One driving factor going into eq3’25 earnings will be broad adoption of Check Point’s AI-powered security features as enterprise adoption of AI becomes the new threat vector. I believe securing an enterprise environment for AI will largely involve a functional Zero Trust platform, the core of tracking identities, both human and machine. In relation to this, Check Point was recognized as a leader for its Zero Trust Platform by Forrester Wave on July 11, 2025 including its coverage across the network, cloud, and endpoint controls for hybrid environments.

Zero Trust has been an area of interest as enterprises adopt agentic AI and GenAI to automate various administrative tasks. The Zero Trust platform is expected to become one of the prominent security features for an enterprise, particularly as enterprises adopt agentic AI to augment personnel. The basis behind this is that AI agents will have access to critical, sensitive data for autonomous use, exposing enterprises to potential threats of agent injection into an environment.

Enhancing its platform, Check Point acquired Veriti in May 2025 to provide AI-driven, real-time threat intelligence enforcement, automated patching, and contextual remediation of security threats. Veriti monitors logs, identifies vulnerabilities, and engages in threat prevention in real time, reducing remediation time and the need for human intervention.

More recently, Check Point announced the acquisition of Lakera on September 16, 2025. Lakera is an AI-native, end-to-end AI security stack for securing agentic AI applications, building on Check Point’s security offerings to protect enterprises during the full lifecycle of AI development and utilization. Lakera’s security solutions provide advanced runtime protection with continuous threat hunting, creating an active security posture for an enterprise. In addition to this, Lakera is powered by Gandalf, a continuous threat intelligence feature that helps detect adversarial patterns.

One feature Check Point is bringing forth is its pay-as-you-go offerings to provide more financial flexibility to customers. As opposed to paying for a subscription upfront, customers can spread the financial burden over time, incentivizing them to utilize more features across the security platform. This was a core transformation for CrowdStrike (CRWD) with its Falcon Flex platform.

Check Point Software Technologies Financial Position

Looking to eq3’25, I’m forecasting Check Point to generate $675mm in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $2.44/share. One of the factors that I will be looking for in the upcoming earnings release is management’s commitment to bolstering its go-to-market initiative to drive future revenue growth. Though reinvesting in sales & marketing may compress margins in the near-term, I believe Check Point can realize improved operating leverage over time as it expands its sales initiative.

Looking at Check Point for the long run, I believe e2h25 will set the tone for future growth with management’s commitment to enhancing the firm’s go-to-market strategy. Depending on the strategy, Check Point may have a stronger runway going into the outer years, particularly as the firm competes with firms like Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and CrowdStrike (CRWD) for multi-nodal AI security.

Risks Related to Check Point Software Technologies

Bull Case

Check Point has a strong market for growth as enterprise AI adoption takes place, potentially adding the need to enhance cybersecurity posture for zero trust. I believe that could realize a strong growth trajectory as it reinvests in expanding its AI capabilities, both for security operations and security posture, though the firm needs to show analysts that it remains committed to bolstering its sales & marketing strategy.

Bear Case

Check Point is operating in a highly competitive market for AI security, potentially posing a threat to its future growth trajectory. Check Point indicated significant discounting to drive platform sales, potentially suggesting that the firm may be at risk of losing market share to other vendors.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Check Point repurchased 1.5mm shares at an average price of $220/share in q2’25, summing to roughly $330mm. Check Point has historically utilized share repurchases to enhance shareholder value; I believe Check Point may continue repurchasing shares going into e2h25 to bolster its earnings per share [EPS].

CHKP shares currently trade at 17.58x EV/aEBITDA, just above the midpoint of its 1-year historical trading range. Taking into consideration its peer comps, CHKP shares are trading at a relative discount, potentially providing room for a higher premium if the firm exhibits improved growth.

Valuing CHKP shares, I believe shares should be priced at $198/share at 17.10x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA; I am recommending CHKP shares with a Buy rating.

We can come up with a similar price range given the low volatility & risk exhibited in CHKP shares. Compared to peer cybersecurity stocks, CHKP has a relatively low 24-month beta of 0.49x, potentially offering a more value-oriented approach to investing in the sector.

Multiples Model Description

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative marketcap and relative multiple.

DCF Model Description

The DCF model can be read with the array of discount rates at the top and terminal growth rates to the left of the model. The forecasted cash flows are denoted at the bottom of the model. The share price array ties the respective discount rates and terminal growth rates. The highlighted prices denoted in the model associate with my expected range for intrinsic value per share.

data.title: Lavoro Cannot Cover Its Cost Of Capital, And The Situation Is Too Hairy
data.description:
Lavoro (NASDAQ:LVRO) is an agchem retailer and manufacturer, operating primarily in Brazil, but also in Colombia and Uruguay.

The company's financial position is critical, as it has not filed financials for the 1H25 calendar operations, has required supplier renegotiation agreements, and was posting large losses in 2H24 calendar (1H25 fiscal). This was generated by large interest expenses on its working capital, added to only operating breakeven profitability since the agchem cycle turned negative in Brazil.

Given the context, I believe the company will need more than it has announced to sustain profitable operations, generating a risk of dilution. In addition, the situation is unclear given the lack of recent financials. For that reason, I initiate Lavoro with a Hold rating.

Brazil and LatAm agchem retailer

Lavoro is a large agchemical retailer and distributor, operating primarily in Brazil.

According to the company's latest 20-F, for FY24 (ended June 2024), the company generates 86% of its revenues in Brazil, with a store network of about 190 stores, and about a thousand sellers. The company also operates retail operations in Colombia and Uruguay, for the remaining 14% of revenues.

In addition, the company has a small manufacturing segment (called Crop Care) that manufactures or imports non-patented products for its retail operations. The segment generated 10% of the company's revenues (mixed between Brazil and other countries).

The company is large in the Brazilian market, but not dominant. According to the 20-F as well (under Brazilian Major Agricultural Input Lines), the Brazilian agchem market was worth R$360 billion, or about $65 billion. Lavoro generated revenues of $1.6 billion in FY24, so a 2.5% market share, approximately. Not small, but obviously not a price setter.

Terrible cycle in agriculture

The company is not an agchem company per se, but rather a commercializer of those products. It is clearly affected by the agchem cycle, but not in the same way as companies that do full production or R&D.
