Still, Lavoro has suffered from a very negative cycle in agriculture after prices fell post-2022, which has led to low farm margins and low investment in agchem products. The cycle was worsened by the fact that crop prices went down a lot more than oil prices, which are a driver of agchem prices.

The cycle affected Lavoro in several ways. Its inventory turnover decreased, increasing the cost of carrying working capital (a fundamental factor in its financial situation, as we will see below). Its margins decreased because inventories bought at higher prices had to be sold at a loss or written down, and in general, the volume of business became too small to sustain its SG&A structure.

Hard to repay the cost of working capital

As we can see above, as of the latest complete financials (September 2024, or 1Q25, more on this later), the company was operationally breakeven.

However, its net losses were very large, up to R$200 million (~$40 million) in 1Q25 alone, because of the interest costs it cannot cover.

The company does not have a lot of debt per se, with 'only' R$1.2 billion in borrowings in 1Q25, versus more than R$500 million in cash. However, the company has to pay interest on its working capital financing, basically its payables to suppliers. This makes up the majority of the company's interest expenses in FY24 and in 1Q25.

The reason is that the cost of capital is not cheap in Brazil. The interbank secured overnight rate (called SELIC) is 15%, meaning a supplier 'lending' inventory to Lavoro is 'losing' the opportunity to earn interest on the money if it charged for the sale upfront.

Indeed, the cost of R$115 million for one quarter of payables (R$600 million annualized) yields an interest rate of about 14% when compared to R$4.2 billion in trade payables in 1Q25.

Financial tightness and restructuring

The company's balance sheet up to 1Q25 was not that bad, given that the company had cash for about R$500 million, and current assets matched current liabilities.

It is true that cash flows were not positive, especially in 1Q25, with a negative R$735 million operationally. However, agrichem companies in Brazil or the Southern Cone tend to build working capital in the first half of the fiscal year (second half of the calendar year) because of the seasonality of crop planting.

For FY24 and FY23 (20-F page 209), operating cash flows had been positive, by R$165 and R$108 million, respectively, inclusive of interest expenses, albeit with a big buildup of payables (R$1 billion higher financing from payables in FY24).

However, in late 2024, the financial situation in the agricultural value chain in Brazil started to get hairy. One of Lavoro's competitors, Agrogalaxy, requested financial bankruptcy in September 2024 (exactly 1Q25, the last complete report from Lavoro), and Brazilian farm loan delinquencies started to surge.

In this context, Lavoro's suppliers started to tighten their conditions and decided not to give more credit to the company. Lavoro stopped filing financials for a long time, until it announced a supplier restructuring agreement in June 2025.

In the release, the company explained that 2Q25 (ended December 2024) had been terrible, with sales falling around 30% in Brazil, and gross profits even more, implying large operational losses (the operating income figure, or income statement, were not provided), because it had no inventory to sell, as suppliers tightened their credit.

The agreement includes the renegotiation of about R$2.5 billion in payables (about 60% of the company's payables in 1Q25), which will be repaid in installments over between 5 and 10 years, indexed to inflation. The company clarified that the suppliers would also continue to provide credit (in the form of merchandise) to the company, but did not comment on how operations had proceeded in the meantime (2H25 fiscal, 1H25 calendar).

In addition, there were no comments about potential benefits to the suppliers (like equity or an interest rate). The conditions reported (inflation-indexing) imply a significant haircut to suppliers (again, imagine that they could earn 15% on the money, versus 4/5% from inflation-indexing).

The situation remains too risky

Again, Lavoro has not filed complete financials for 2Q25 (we don't know how much it lost from operations), nor for the 2H25 fiscal period, all of which also happened without an agreement with suppliers.

Losses could be very large, assuming a 30% haircut to revenues for the whole fiscal year, and flat SG&A, we could be talking of operational losses of as much as $75 million: $240 million in gross profits in FY24 by 70% becomes $175 million, minus $250 million in SG&A is $75 million in operating losses.

We do not know the new shape of the balance sheet, how much cash is left, or the damage to customer relations from not being able to serve them. The potential operational loss above would almost wipe out the R$500 million (less than $100 million) in cash the company had.

Only from this perspective, the company is simply too risky for investment. It would be basically like buying a lottery ticket without knowing the payouts or the probabilities.

Further, the company will need massive capitalization even beyond the payables restructuring. During the best period in its reported history, the company generated about $65 million in operating profitability. This was during the peak cycle for crops, during the post-pandemic + Ukraine war boom.

At that time, the company was already unable to cover its financial structure. That simply means the company is unable to cover its cost of capital if it comes from debt (its return on assets is below the cost of financing).

It would not face this problem with equity, which has only a residual claim, although it is possible that equity financers would simply earn a low return.

That is, even assuming an instantaneous recovery to the best position of the cycle, the company would still be unprofitable without financing. This implies high risks for equity, either from bankruptcy (a restructuring with suppliers is very close to that) or from dilution.

For those reasons, I initiate Lavoro with a Hold rating, waiting for more news.

data.title: TAL Education: Turning Positive On Potential Q2 Beat And Generous Capital Returns (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've become bullish on TAL Education Group (NYSE:TAL).

The analysts' conservative financial projections are setting up the stage for a 2QFY2026 (YE end-Feb '26) beat. Concerns about potential cash hoarding are gradually being alleviated, as it has been doing aggressive buybacks.

TAL was previously a Hold-rated name. The prior April 21, 2025 article outlined my mixed view of its expected Q4FY2025 showing and the US-China trade war effects.

Anticipating A Positive Surprise For 2Q

The firm is revealing its latest financials closer to end-Oct.

I think that the sell-side is too pessimistic about TAL.

The consensus 2QFY26 (June 1-Aug 31, 2025) sales and normalized EPS growth rates for the company are +33.8% and +33.9%, respectively on YoY terms.

It had done much better in the preceding quarter.

TAL's turnover rose 38.8% year-on-year to $0.58B in 1QFY2026. During the same period, its bottom-line was 40.0% higher at $0.07.

My take is that it will deliver above-consensus results in two weeks' time.

A leading indicator of future performance is "deferred revenue."

TAL explained in its 20-F that the group's "learning fee" is "collected in advance and is initially recorded as deferred revenue" before being "recognized proportionately as the learning sessions are delivered."

The enterprise's "deferred revenue" jumped +52% YoY in 1QFY26. This has a favorable read-across for the current fiscal year's financial outlook.

Separately, its education-related devices have been selling well.

Goldman Sachs (GS) issued a research note (inaccessible in public) titled "China Education Aug-25 Tracker" on Sep 15. TAL's "Gross Merchandise Value" or "GMV" increase for "learning tablets" improved from 1QFY2026's 65% to 2QFY26's 79% based on GS's evaluation of e-commerce portal data.

I'm forecasting that its Q2 top-line goes up by an above-expectations +39%, taking into account the encouraging numbers presented above.

Also, my predictions point to TAL's normalized EBIT-to-sales metric widening from 10.4% for 2QFY25 to 12.0% in June-Aug this year.

It stressed at the first quarter call in end-July that boosting "overall profitability is one of the company's key priorities." "Operating leverage", "refining our operations at different stages of our workflow", and capitalizing on "today's technology and driving overall efficiency" were cited as TAL's key cost levers according to its results briefing commentary.

I estimate TAL will register a +42% EPS growth for 2QFY2026, thanks to better-than-anticipated revenues and operating margin enhancement.

It's highly probable that the share price will respond favorably to a 2nd quarter beat.

A Richer Valuation Is Warranted For Its Substantial Cash Distributions

TAL is now trading at a modest 1.15x next twelve months' "Enterprise Value-to-Sales" or "EV/S." It also boasts an undemanding ex-cash FY27 P/E of 9.3x. These figures were taken from S&P Capital IQ.

My opinion is that it hasn't been sufficiently rewarded by investors for its shareholder-friendly capital allocation moves.

TAL bought $0.48B worth of its own shares in the Apr-July '25 time frame. This represents a meaningful 7% of its market cap. It is non-dividend paying.

It had net cash amounting to $3.1B on its books as of end-1QFY26. The group has shown an intent to tackle the "lazy balance sheet" issue with the return of excess capital.

TAL's new one-year $0.6B repurchase program was initiated in late-July. That translates into a potential buyback yield of 9%.

Its peer New Oriental Education (EDU) is valued by the market at a comparable forward EV/S of 1.09x.

EDU's net-cash stands at a significant $3.8B (source: S&P Capital IQ). But its expected FY26 (YE May) cash distribution is less than $300M based on its plan to share "50% of the company's net income" with its stockholders. This is below its actual FY25's dividends and repurchases of $0.5B.

TAL's buybacks for Q1FY26 alone ($480M) have already exceeded what EDU is anticipated to pay out in the full year.

TAL is deserving of a valuation premium, given that the company's shareholder return approach has been superior than its peer.

I think that stock can rise to 1.5x EV/S, which is pegged to its three-year historical mean.

You Should Watch These Risks

A 2QFY26 miss could lead to a valuation de-rating and stock price correction for TAL. This can occur in the event of a surge in expenses or slower-than-expected revenue recognition.

The firm might take a pause on buying back its own stock, if it focuses on other cash allocation alternatives such as M&A. Yield-focused investors are likely to be less interested in TAL under that scenario.

My Conclusion

TAL's rating is lifted from a Hold to a Buy.

I see above-consensus Q2 numbers and continued repurchases as re-rating triggers.

Its EV/S multiple can potentially expand by +30% (15/1.15) with my target of 1.5 times mentioned above.
